coredata.dc:title: Solution-processed ZnO:graphene quantum dot/Poly-TPD heterojunction for high-performance UV photodetectors
coredata.dc:creator: Hoang Tran, Manh
Park, Taehyun
Hur, Jaehyun
coredata.dc:description:
Recently, ZnO-based photodetectors have gained immense attention for ultraviolet detection applications owing to their abundance and cost-effective synthesis. However, low responsivity, slow speed, and complicated fabrication limit the applications of these detectors. Herein, we report a high-performance UV photodiode based on n-type graphene quantum dot-decorated ZnO (ZnO:GQD) and p-type poly(N,N’-bis-4-butylphenyl-N,N’-bisphenyl)benzidine (Poly-TPD) heterojunction. First, we demonstrate the incorporation of GQD in ZnO not only reduces the ZnO surface roughness and ZnO nanoparticle size but also promotes the transport of photogenerated carriers and increases the charge lifetime of ZnO. The effect of GQD content on the physical and chemical characteristics of the ZnO film is investigated using Raman spectroscopy, XRD, SEM, AFM, UV–vis spectroscopy, and PL. Second, under the appropriate GQD concentration condition, the performances of various ZnO:GQD-based devices are compared to understand the charge transport mechanism. As a result, the photodiode based on ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD shows the most balanced energy structure, enhanced mobility, and alleviation in charge recombination relative to the other devices. The ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD photodetector exhibits excellent performance under 365-nm UV illumination: a rise/decay time of 0.37/0.78 s and a specific
d e t e c t i v i t y
o f
2.1 ×
10
11
Jones (at −3 V bias) with outstanding stability at least for 10 weeks without significant photocurrent degeneration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Zinc oxide
Poly-TPD
UV
Photodetector

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20194030202290). This research was supported by the Korea Electric Power Corporation (Grant number: R18XA02).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis (a) and PL (b) spectra of the GQD-3 solution.
Raman spectra at the excitation wavelength of 633 nm (a) and XRD patterns (b) of the ZnO and ZnO:GQD-3 thin films. AFM, SEM, and elemental mapping images of the ZnO (c), and ZnO:GQD-3 (d) thin films.

UV–Vis (a) and PL spectra (at the excitation wavelength of 325 nm) (b) of the ZnO and ZnO:GQD films. Schematic illustration of the effect of incorporating GQD on the band diagram of ZnO (c). TRPL spectra (at the excitation wavelength of 316 nm) of ZnO and ZnO:GQD-3 films (d).
Operating mechanisms of the p-n heterojunction (a) and conductive detector (b). Energy band diagrams of various p-n photodiodes [47–50] (c).

I-V curves of the devices with the ITO/ZnO/Poly-TPD/Ag(a), ITO/ZnO:GQD-1/Poly-TPD/Ag (b), ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag (c), and ITO/ZnO:GQD-5/Poly-TPD/Ag (d) structures under the dark and 365-nm UV irradiation (0.59 mW cm−2) conditions. Responsivity and specific detectivity (e), and Nyquist plots in the range of 5 mHz –5 kHz (f) of the devices at −3 V bias.

FIB-SEM image of (a), spectral response (b) of the ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag device. Photoresponse (the inset shows the absolute current under UV radiation as a function of light intensity) (c), responsivity and specific detectivity (inset shows I-V curves) (d), and EQE (e) of the ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag device at an applied voltage of −3 V under 365-nm illumination over the intensity range of 0.37–1.2 mW cm−2. The photoresponses of the device in the beginning (black) and after 10 weeks for 100 cycles (red) at 0.59 mW cm−2 under 365-nm UV illumination (the inset shows the photocurrent level of the device measured after different cycles) (f).
Performance of the ZnO-based UV photodetector developed in this study and those reported previously.

head.ce:title: Solution-processed ZnO:graphene quantum dot/Poly-TPD heterojunction for high-performance UV photodetectors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, Seongnam, Gyeonggi 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, Seongnam, Gyeonggi 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Recently, ZnO-based photodetectors have gained immense attention for ultraviolet detection applications owing to their abundance and cost-effective synthesis. However, low responsivity, slow speed, and complicated fabrication limit the applications of these detectors. Herein, we report a high-performance UV photodiode based on n-type graphene quantum dot-decorated ZnO (ZnO:GQD) and p-type poly(N,N’-bis-4-butylphenyl-N,N’-bisphenyl)benzidine (Poly-TPD) heterojunction. First, we demonstrate the incorporation of GQD in ZnO not only reduces the ZnO surface roughness and ZnO nanoparticle size but also promotes the transport of photogenerated carriers and increases the charge lifetime of ZnO. The effect of GQD content on the physical and chemical characteristics of the ZnO film is investigated using Raman spectroscopy, XRD, SEM, AFM, UV–vis spectroscopy, and PL. Second, under the appropriate GQD concentration condition, the performances of various ZnO:GQD-based devices are compared to understand the charge transport mechanism. As a result, the photodiode based on ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD shows the most balanced energy structure, enhanced mobility, and alleviation in charge recombination relative to the other devices. The ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD photodetector exhibits excellent performance under 365-nm UV illumination: a rise/decay time of 0.37/0.78 s and a specific
d e t e c t i v i t y
o f
2.1 ×
10
11
Jones (at −3 V bias) with outstanding stability at least for 10 weeks without significant photocurrent degeneration.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) detector has gained tremendous attention owing to its applications, including flame detection, biological sensing, environmental sensing, and space communications [1]. An ideal UV detector should satisfy the requirements of high responsivity, low noise, high spectral selectivity, repeatability with low-cost and simple fabrication process. Unlike heavy-weight vacuum UV detectors which require a high-pressure operation (e.g., photomultiplier tubes), semiconductor-based detectors operated via the photoelectric effect are suitable for portable applications because of their low-power consumption [1]. These detectors can be classified into two categories: detectors based on narrow and wide bandgap semiconductors. Detectors based on narrow bandgap semiconductors such as Si diode detectors are equipped with high-cost UV-pass filters [2,3]. Unfortunately, the costly production of purified Si and its high absorption coefficient, which prevents the photogenerated carriers from reaching the depletion layer, limit the applications of Si photodiodes [4,5]. On the other hand, detectors based on wide bandgap semiconductors usually use metal oxides such as ZnO, TiO2, SnO2, and Ga2O3 [6–9].

ZnO, which exhibits a large bandgap of 3.37 eV, a strong radiation hardness with a threshold displacement energy of up to 30 eV (e.g., by far higher than 9 eV of GaAs and 14 eV of Si) [10], and high chemical stability, is a promising and abundant candidate for optoelectronic devices operable in the UV spectral range [3,6]. ZnO is intrinsically an n-type semiconductor, but the introduction of other metals or semiconductors in ZnO can improve the electrical properties of ZnO-based devices [11–14].

In recent years, semiconducting graphene quantum dots (GQDs), considered as zero-dimensional graphene sheets with a size of several-nanometers and strong optical absorption in the UV/visible (Vis) range, have gained enormous scientific attention for applications in light-emitting diodes (LEDs), photo-electrochemical water splitting, and photovoltaic devices [15–17]. The decoration of GQD in ZnO can open new revenues for developing high performance UV sensors because it can result in the formation of novel interfaces, which can enhance the separation and transportation of photogenerated carriers, thus, improving the photoresponse of the detectors [18–22]. GQDs can be easily decorated on ZnO films because of their remarkably small spatial size and n-type semiconducting nature [23]. Furthermore, GQDs exhibit high electron mobility, which can facilitate charge carrier transfer in the ZnO lattice. In addition, GQDs can prevent the recombination of electrons and holes in the ZnO lattice [24].

In addition to the employment of new element in ZnO, the device configuration can also strongly affect the performance of UV photodetectors. ZnO-based UV detectors are largely of three types: photoconductor, Schottky junction, and p-n junction detectors. First, it is easy to fabricate photoconductor detectors because of their simple structures. However, these detectors suffered from slow response speed (up to above 50 s) and high dark current [25]. Second, Schottky junction and double back-to-back Schottky junction (also called metal–semiconductor-metal, MSM) detectors exhibit low capacitance per unit area, which endow them with a fast response time but the difficulty in controlling metal–semiconductor interface to avoid shadowing effect of the metal electrode limited their practical applications [2]. Lastly, p-n junction detectors can serve as photodiodes with low dark current, fast response, and high stability [26]. In this context, the formation of p-n heterojunction between GQD-ZnO with an appropriate p-type material should give the new promise for the high-performance UV photodetectors. Poly(N,Ń́́ʹ-bis-4-butylphenyl-N,Nʹ-bisphenyl) benzidine (Poly-TPD) is an excellent p-type polymer that has been widely used in organic light-emitting diodes owing to its large optical absorption bandwidth and high sensitivity [27–29]. With the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) level of −5.2 eV for Poly-TPD, the heterojunction between GQD-ZnO and Poly-TPD (namely, ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD) can offer the great opportunity for the high-performance UV photodetector.

In the present study, we demonstrate a new p-n heterojunction based on the ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD to realize the high-performance UV photodetectors with fast photoresponse speed, and dramatically enhanced photoresponsivity. Although the solution-processed ZnO-based UV photodetectors have been widely reported [30–32], it has never been considered to introduce GQDs to ZnO and formation of ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD p-n heterojunction for the enhanced performance of UV detectors. First, ZnO:GQD thin films with different GQD content were deposited on an indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated quartz substrate using the sol–gel spin coating method. Second, with an appropriate GQD concentration, the device structure was further optimized with respect to different p-type polymers. The ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD junction possesses the following advantages; (i) the incorporation of GQD in ZnO facilitates the charge transport and extend the carrier lifetime relative to bare ZnO, (ii) the formation of ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD heterojunction reduces the hole injection barrier which promotes the charge carriers movement without much recombination, (iii) the high mobility of Poly-TPD allows the fast photoresponse for ZnO-based UV detectors. As a result, the photodiode with ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD exhibited a high specific detectivity of 2.1 × 1011 Jones and a fast rise/decay time of 0.37/0.78 s at an applied bias of −3 V under 365-nm UV illumination. Furthermore, the device exhibits good stability with a trivial change in the photoresponse after 10 weeks, thus demonstrating good potential for UV detection applications.

GQDs were synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method using graphene oxide (GO, 95% Graphenea) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt% Daejung). Briefly, dried GO (50, 150, and 250 mg) was dispersed into 10 mL of H2O2 by sonication for 30 min. The resulting dispersion was then diluted with 40 mL of H2O2 to obtain a GO suspension (1, 3, and 5 mg mL−1, respectively). This suspension was stirred for 15 min at 25 °C and was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. As hydrogen peroxide was consumed and decomposed completely after the reaction, the resulting product was an aqueous solution of GQDs. The solutions with the GQD concentrations of 1, 3, and 5 mg mL−1 were labeled as GQD-1, GQD-3, and GQD-5, respectively.

The ZnO:GQD thin films were fabricated by a sol–gel spin coating method using zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, ZA, 99.999% Aldrich) as the precursor. First, 220 mg of ZA was dissolved into 3.85 mL of isopropanol (IPA, 99.7% Aldrich) containing 5 mg mL−1 polyethylenimine (PEI, branched, Mw of 25000, Aldrich). Then, 450 μL of deionized (DI) water was added to the resulting solution followed by stirring at 25 °C for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous mixture. The viscous precursor solution was coated on an ITO-coated 1 mm-thick quartz substrate (quartz/ITO size: 15 mm × 15 mm/11 mm × 15 mm, a resistivity of 15 Ω/sq, Korea Nanomaterials) by spin-coating at 3000 rpm for 20 s, followed by annealing at 400 °C for 1 h to form a ZnO thin film. The ZnO:GQD-1, ZnO:GQD-3, and ZnO:GQD-5 thin films were obtained by replacing 450 μL of DI water with 450 μL of the GQD-1, GQD-3, and GQD-5 solutions, respectively, in the synthesis procedure.

The UV photodetectors were fabricated as illustrated in Fig. S1. On the ZnO-based n-type film, a p-type layer was fabricated by spin-coating a 15 mg mL−1 solution of Poly-TPD (Ossila) in chlorobenzene at 2000 rpm for 40 s. The resulting assembly was then placed on a hot plate at 110 °C for 20 min. Finally, a 100 nm-thick Ag layer was deposited on the top of the Poly-TPD film as the top electrode using a thermal evaporation system (STM-100/MF Sycon instrument, USA). A deposition rate of 2 Å s−1 and a high-vacuum pressure of 8 × 10−6 Torr was used. The area of the overlapped active region in the UV photodetector was 0.1656 cm2.

The presence of GQDs in the ZnO:GQD film was confirmed using micro-Raman spectroscopy (Olympus BX51, USA) with a He-Ne laser excitation wavelength of 633 nm. The crystal structures of the ZnO and ZnO:GQD films were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD; Rigaku DMAX 2200, Japan) using a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.506 Å) at a scan rate of 5° min−1. The thermal decomposition of the ZnO:GQD films was investigated by carrying out their thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (SDT Q600, TA Instruments (Germany)) measurements by heating them to 800 °C under an air atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The morphologies of the films were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi S-4700, Japan), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; JEOL JEM-2100F, USA), and atomic force microscopy (AFM; Veeco, USA) (scan rate of 0.5 Hz in the tapping mode). The thicknesses of the constituent layers of the device were determined using focused ion beam-scanning electron microscopy (FIB-SEM; S-4700, Hitachi, Japan at Smart Materials Research Center for IoT (Gachon University)). The UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the ZnO and ZnO:GQD films deposited on the quartz substrates were recorded using Agilent 8453 (USA) and QuantaMaster TM 50 PTI (USA) systems, respectively. The effect of GQD adding on carrier lifetime of ZnO film was probed by time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL; FLS920 Horiba, Japan) measurement. The conductivity values of ZnO and ZnO:GQD films were determined at room temperature by four-probe resistivity method (CMT-SR2000 N, South Korea). A 365-nm UV lamp (VL-6.LC, France) was used to provide simulated UV irradiation. The response photocurrent of the sensors was recorded using a Keithley source meter instrument (2400, USA). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement for evaluating charge transportation of devices was conducted by the potentiostat–galvanostat (VSP, Bio-Logic Science Instruments, France). The spectral response of the optimized device was derived using solar cell quantum efficiency test system (McScience K3100, Korea).

The UV–Vis spectrum of the as-prepared GQD-3 solution (Fig. 1 a) showed a clear absorption peak at 220 nm and a broad shoulder peak at 325 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of C = C bonds and the n-π* transition of C = O bonds, respectively, confirming the successful synthesis of GQDs via the solvothermal method [24,33]. The bandgap of the GQD-3 solution was 3.67 eV, as estimated from its Tauc plot (inset in Fig. 1a). This is consistent with the values reported previously [24]. Fig. 1b shows the PL emission spectrum of the GQD-3 solution at the excitation wavelength of 275–425 nm. The emission peak intensity increased with a decrease in the excitation wavelength from 425 to 325 nm and reached a maximum value at the excitation wavelength of 325 nm. This is consistent with the absorption shoulder peak observed at 325 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 1a). This emission peak corresponds to the radiative recombination from Oπ* to Cπ (Fig. S2a). Meanwhile, the two broad emission peaks observed at the UV wavelengths shorter than 325 nm (the emission peaks at the excitation wavelengths of 275 and 300 nm in Fig. 1b) correspond to the recombination of excited electrons in two ways (Fig. S2b): (i) band-to-band recombination (Cπ* → Cπ); (ii) intersystem crossing (Cπ* → Oπ*) followed by the transition from Oπ* to Cπ [34]. These PL characteristics of the synthesized GQDs are consistent with the optical properties of GQDs reported previously [34]. These results suggest that GQDs are efficient sensitizer for ZnO thin films for UV photodetector applications.

The TGA-DSC results of the ZA:GQD film (ZnO:GQD precursor film) are shown in Fig. S3. The TGA-DSC curve of this film showed four mass loss stages at: (I) 100–200 °C attributing to the desorption and evaporation of the solvent in ZA:GQD, (II) 200–400 °C attributing to the thermal decomposition of ZA and its transition to ZnO ((Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O
→ Δ
Zn4O(CH3COO)6
→ Δ
ZnO) along with the release of CH3COOH and CO2 [35]), (III) 400–530 °C attributing to the decomposition of PEI in the precursor solution, and (IV) 530–560 °C attributing to the thermal degradation of the GQDs. Therefore, the optimum annealing temperature for the formation of ZnO without the degradation of the GQDs and PEI was found to be ~ 400 °C. The ZnO:GQD thin film formation was proposed as shown in Fig. S4. As reported previously, the PEI reduces the electron trapping in ZnO and a series resistance [36]. This is based on the high [N + ]/[C] ratio in PEI that induces strong electrostatic or dipole–dipole interactions with ZnO surface. As a result, ZnO is aligned perpendicular to the substrate, facilitating the vertical transport of electrons [37]. The presence of GQDs in the ZnO:GQD film was confirmed by Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectrum of ZnO:GQD (Fig. 2 a) showed two peaks at 1365 (D band) and 1585 cm−1 (G band) corresponding to the disordered structure and sp2 conjugated carbon network of the GQDs, respectively [24]. As can be observed from Fig. 2b, the ZnO and ZnO:GQD films showed diffraction peaks at 2θ = ~30.9, 33.7, 35.4, 46.7, 56, 62.2, 67.3, and 68.4° corresponding to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112), and (201) planes of hexagonal crystalline ZnO (JCPDS, no. 76–0704). As compared to the ZnO thin film, the ZnO:GQD film showed no additional diffraction peaks, indicating that the combination of GQD did not change the crystal structure of ZnO. However, the presence of GQDs (the diameter of GQDs measured to be 4–5 nm from the separate HRTEM image, Fig. S5) throughout ZnO formation resulted in preventing the aggregation of ZnO particles, thus decreasing the particle size of ZnO:GQD. This was also confirmed by the AFM and SEM results (Fig. 2c and 2d). The AFM results revealed that the surface roughness of ZnO decreased (from 3.022 nm (ZnO) to 2.127 nm (ZnO:GQD) of root mean square - Rq values) with the incorporation of GQDs in ZnO. The SEM results revealed that the incorporation of GQDs reduced the particle size of ZnO (from ~ 50 nm (ZnO) to below 20 nm (ZnO:GQD)). Nevertheless, both the ZnO and ZnO:GQD films showed a uniform distribution of Zn and O from EDX mapping images.

The UV–Vis and PL spectra of the ZnO and ZnO:GQD (with different GQD concentrations) films are shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b, respectively. Bandgap (Eg) values of ZnO and ZnO:GQD films were estimated by extrapolating the straight portion of Tauc plots (the inset in Fig. 3a), which can be expressed as (αhν)2 = A(hν-Eg), where A is a constant, α is the absorption coefficient, and hν is photon energy [24]. The ZnO film showed a bandgap of 3.23 eV, consistent with the bandgap of ZnO nanoparticles (3.20–3.32 eV) synthesized in previous studies [38]. The ZnO thin film showed smaller bandgap than bulk ZnO (~3.37 eV) because of the presence of intrinsic defects and vacancies (Fig. 3b). It can be observed from Fig. 3a that the UV absorption of ZnO enhanced significantly (especially in UV-B range) with the addition of the GQDs. The ZnO:GQD-3 film showed the maximum absorbance in all the UV regions with an increase in the bandgap to the maximum value (3.37 eV, inset of Fig. 3a) as compared to the bare ZnO (3.23 eV) film. This suggests that the UV detection response of the bare ZnO film improved with the incorporation of GQDs. However, excessive introduction of GQD (ZnO:GQD-5) forms a new energy state (a new balance band) in ZnO, leading to the decrease in the bandgap (3.20 eV in Fig. 3c). The reduced bandgap of ZnO:GQD-5 is revealed in Tauc plot (inset in Fig. 3a) which is extracted from UV–vis absorbance. Consequently, the UV–vis absorbance for ZnO:GQD-5 is also significantly decreased (Fig. 3a). PL measurements were carried out to investigate the effect of GQD incorporation on the optical properties of ZnO. As can be observed from Fig. 3b, the ZnO:GQD films showed three intense peaks at 370, 422, and 468 nm at the excitation wavelength of 325 nm corresponding to the characteristic near band-edge emission (NBE) of ZnO, electron transition from the ZnO conduction band (CB) to the GQD valence band (VB), and structural defects and vacancies, respectively [39,40]. The NBE peak intensity of the ZnO:GQD films was significantly lower than that of the bare ZnO film. On the other hand, the PL intensities of the films at 422 nm increased with the increase in the GQD concentration. On the basis of these UV–Vis and PL spectrum results, the energy band diagrams of the ZnO:GQD films can be explained (Fig. 3c). The incorporation of GQD affected ZnO thin film in two ways simultaneously. One is decreasing ZnO nanoparticles size (as observed from the SEM images), which causes the increase in bandgap of ZnO:GQD films as compared with the bare ZnO film via quantum confinement effect, as consistent with previous studies [41,42]. Concurrently, the GQDs introduced additional energy states right above the ZnO VB, which captures the photogenerated electrons to alleviate the radiative recombination. As a result, a large number of electrons existed for a longer time, increasing the circuit current under UV excitation [24]. This was confirmed by the increase in the intensity of the PL peak at 422 nm with the incorporation of GQDs. This increase in the PL peak intensity directly reflects the electron transition from the ZnO CB to the GQD VB. The charge recombination suppression ability of the ZnO:GQD films increased with an increase in the GQD concentration from 1 to 5 mg mL−1. However, excessive GQDs in ZnO:GQD-5 formed a large number of Zn-O-C bonds, which is attributed to the intersystem crossings (i. e. charge transitions between energy bands of ZnO and GQD) as depicted in Fig. 3c, thus, practically reduced the bandgap of ZnO (3.20 eV). This phenomenon was also reported in previous researches [38,43,44]. Hence, it can be stated that an optimum concentration of GQD in ZnO:GQD (i.e., ZnO:GQD-3 in our work) satisfies the requirements of charge recombination suppression and the elevation of the ZnO bandgap simultaneously. TRPL measurement was performed to further probe the prolonged lifetime carriers in ZnO:GQD film as shown in Fig. 3d. The average decay time in ZnO and ZnO:GQD-3 films was 0.37 and 0.41 ns, respectively, derived from fitting 3-exponentials decay function. This increased carrier lifetime in ZnO:GQD implies a larger number of separated photocarriers for the charge collection at electrodes relative to bare ZnO [45].

After determining the optimum conditions for GQD concentration for ZnO film, we further investigated the optimal photodetector structures by considering four model devices using ZnO:GQD-3: ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag, ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Ag, ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/ poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS)/Ag, and ITO/ZnO/Poly-TPD/Ag (Fig. S6). The ZnO:GQD-3 thin film was applied to the p-n heterojunction photodiode (ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag). In addition, ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Ag, ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/PEDOT:PSS/Ag, and ITO/ZnO/Poly-TPD/Ag were prepared as control devices for comparison. The transient photoresponses of the ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Ag and ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag detectors at −3 V bias under 365-nm UV illumination revealed that the photoresponse behavior of the p-n heterojunction device (Fig. S7a) was superior to that of the photoconductor device (Fig. S7b). The rise/decay times of Poly-TPD/ZnO:GQD-3 photodiode (0.37/0.78 s) were much smaller than those of ZnO:GQD-3 photoconductor (6.6/7.0 s). As schematically explained in Fig. 4 a, the width of the depletion layer is defined by the intrinsic nature of the p-n interface, which is widened under an applied reverse bias in the photodiode architecture (ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag). Upon UV illumination, electron-hole pairs were formed in the ZnO:GQD film in the depletion region and dissociated into electrons and holes by the electric field. These electron and holes then moved rapidly towards each electrode in opposite directions under the reverse bias. On the other hand, the operating mechanism of the photoconductor with the ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Ag structure (without p-type material) was based on the sluggish oxygen adsorption/desorption regulated by UV irradiation (Fig. 4b). Briefly, ambient oxygen molecules adsorbed on the ZnO surface in the form of O2 – ions decreased the electrical conductivity of ZnO in the dark, which is independent of ZnO morphologies [25,46]. Upon UV excitation, ZnO:GQD generated electron-hole pairs. Then, the O2 – ions from ZnO combined with oppositely charged holes to form neutral O2 gas, resulting in the formation of free electrons as major carriers under the external bias. The photocurrent generated in this process was very high (up to three-orders of magnitude higher than that in the case of the Poly-TPD/ZnO:GQD p-n heterojunction structure). This is because the active surface area of the photoconductor that was larger than the depletion region of the photodiode. However, the response speed of the photoconductors was very low because of the slow oxygen adsorption/desorption on the ZnO:GQD surface (Fig. S7b). By comparing the performance of the devices with Poly-TPD and PEDOT:PSS (AL4083 Ossila) (at the same concentration), which is a hole transfer material (HTMs) widely used in thin-film organic electronic devices, it can be stated that the choice of the p-type polymer significantly affects the performance of a p-n detector (Figs. S7a and S7c).

The energy band diagrams of the photodiode devices and the HOMO/LUMO levels of PEDOT:PSS, Poly-TPD, ZnO, and the GQDs are shown in Fig. 4c [47–50]. The energy barrier between the VB edge of ZnO:GQD and PEDOT:PSS was 2.4 eV. On the other hand, when Poly-TPD was used, the hole-injection barrier reduced by 0.3 eV, suggesting that the photogenerated holes could be easily injected from the VB of ZnO:GQD to that of Poly-TPD [50]. Moreover, the hole mobility of Poly-TPD (1 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1 [28]) is higher than that of PEDOT:PSS (5.55 × 10−5 cm2V−1 s−1 [47]). This can be another reason for the superior photoresponse behavior of the ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD junction to that of the ZnO:GQD/PEDOT:PSS junction as showed in Fig. S7c. Therefore, the detector with Poly-TPD as the p-type semiconductor showed better performance (higher photocurrent and response speed) than the detector with PEDOT:PSS. Taking into account of photoresponse time of the optimum device (Fig. S7a), the fast and slow rise/decay time constants (τ1 rise and τ2 rise/τ1 decay and τ2 decay) were determined by fitting a bi-exponential growth and decay equations [24,51]:
(1) I(t) = I0 A1 exp(t/τ1) A2 exp(t/τ2)

where I(t) is current at time t, I0 is the asymptotic current, A1 and A2 are constants. As shown in Fig. S8, the fast process (34/36 ms of τ1 rise/τ1 decay) and the slow process (1.140/1.635 s of τ2 rise/τ2 decay) were ascribed to photoresponse from the p-n depletion and the ZnO:GQD region outside p-n junction, respectively. These values are much lower than GQD/ZnO-based devices using other concepts (e.g., photoconductive and Schottky detectors) reported formerly [24,51], proposing an excellent UV-sensing device obtained. In addition, ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag showed a much higher on-current level (by a factor of ~ 4) than ITO/ZnO/Poly-TPD/Ag (without GQD decoration in ZnO).

Fig. 5 a-e shows the photoresponses, responsivity, and specific detectivity of the photodiodes (I-V curves) using ZnO, ZnO:GQD-1, ZnO:GQD-3, and ZnO:GQD-5 as the n-type semiconductors and Poly-TPD as the p-type polymer under 365-nm UV illumination. The responsivity and specific detectivity values of the devices could be calculated from their I-V curves using the following equations [32]:

R e s p o n s i v i t y
R
=
I
l i g h t
-
I
d a r k
P
o p t
(2)

S p e c i f i c
d e t e c t i v i t y
( D
∗
)
=
R
2 e
J
d a r k
0.5
(3)

where Ilight is the current under 365-nm UV illumination, Idark is the dark current, Popt is the incident optical power, e is the electron charge, and Jdark is the dark current density. All the devices showed a diode-like rectifying behavior over the bias range of −3–3 V. Under the reverse bias of −3–0 V, the device with GQD-3 showed significantly higher photocurrent under UV illumination than the other devices. The high photocurrent of this device led to an improved detector responsivity, as shown in Fig. 5e. Thus, the on/off ratio (ratio of current under illumination to dark current) of the device with the GQDs (ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag) (Fig. S7a) was as high as 12, which is ~ 6 times higher than that of device without the GQDs (ITO/ZnO/Poly-TPD/Ag) (Fig. S7d). I-V curves of an ITO/GQDs/Poly-TPD/Ag device (GQDs film was prepared following the synthetic procedure of ZnO:GQD-3 film except for adding ZA) under dark and 365-nm illumination are shown in Fig. S9. The results showed that there is almost negligible photocurrent for ITO/GQDs/Poly-TPD/Ag devices. This means that GQD itself cannot generate the meaningful photocurrent without ZnO; thus indicating that GQD layer can only act as charge carriers transporting layer for ZnO. It should be noted that the small amount of GQDs present at ZnO:GQD-3 film hardly participate in the photocurrent generation because of its low absorption of 365 nm UV (Fig. 1a). In spite of that, the VB of the GQDs (−6.56 eV) located between those of ZnO (−7.5 eV) and Poly-TPD (−5.4 eV) as shown in Fig. 4c facilitated hole extraction in the devices with the GQDs. Consequently, the average decay time of ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD junction (3.8 ns) was significantly lower than that of the ZnO/Poly-TPD junction (4.3 ns) (Fig. S10). Besides, the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) values of devices with and without GQD-3 introduction were estimated to be 38 and 62 Ω cm2, respectively, from semicircle diameters in Nyquist plots (Fig. 5f). From the separate measurement of conductivity using four-probe measurement, the conductivities of ZnO and ZnO:GQD were 4.1 × 10−5 and 5.2 × 10−5 S cm−1, respectively. Therefore, the significant decrease in Rct for ZnO:GQD, suggesting an improved charge transportation, is ascribed to the higher conductivity of ZnO:GQD than that of bare ZnO film, resulted from high conductivity of GQDs (~1.8 × 10−3 S cm−1 [52]). However, in the case of ZnO:GQD-5, a large number of GQDs should be inevitably present outside the depletion region. These GQDs show strong absorbance in the UV range, and hence reduce the amount of the UV radiation detected in the active p-n junction. Therefore, the excessive GQDs in the device using ZnO:GQD-5 resulted in an unduly high dark current and a decrease in the detectivity (Fig. 5d and e). It is of note that Fig. S11 reflected the statistics of characteristic parameters (Ilight, R, and D) from two batches of ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD and bare ZnO/Poly-TPD (each batch has 10 devices) revealed a good reproducibility of the sensors.

The FIB-SEM image of the ITO/ZnO:GQD-3/Poly-TPD/Ag device is shown in Fig. 6 a. It shows the thickness of the layers used in the device. The thickness of the ZnO:GQD film should be sufficiently low (in our device the thickness of ZnO:GQD was 100 nm) for the UV light to be fully in contact with the depletion region of the p-n junction [32]. The spectral responsivity of ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD detector (Fig. 6b) revealed the selective detection of UV range (300–420 nm) with a maximum detectable wavelength of ~ 420 nm, which is consistent with UV–vis spectrum of ZnO:GQD thin film (Fig. 3a). To investigate the effect of the light power intensity on the photodetector response, the performance of the optimized device (in terms of I-V and I-t measurements) using ZnO:GQD-3 and Poly-TPD was quantitatively assessed in terms of responsivity, specific detectivity, and external quantum efficiency (EQE). The EQE of the device could be calculated using the following equation [53]:

E Q E = R
h c
e λ
(4)
where R is the responsivity, h is the Planck constant, c is the speed of light, and λ is the wavelength of the irradiated light (365 nm).

As shown in Fig. 6c, the photocurrent was proportional to the intensity of the incident UV radiation. With an increase in the light intensity from 0.37 to 1.04 mW cm−2, the photoinduced current linearly increased from 45 to 80 μA because an increase in the photoflux density resulted in an increase in the number of photogenerated charge carriers, which drifted towards the electrodes generating a high output current. The values of R, D, and EQE decreased with an increase in the incident intensity (Fig. 6d and 6e). This can be attributed to the saturation of carrier-traps in the active region with an increase in the number of charges generated by high-power UV irradiation [54]. More specifically, when the light with relatively weak intensity reached the active junction, a considerable number of photoexcited carriers were captured by the defect and GQD traps in ZnO:GQD in the longer time with higher current response, as can be observed from the PL results. With an increase in the UV light intensity, the traps gradually filled and the number of carriers that could not be trapped increased, leading to a decrease in the average carrier lifetime. Consequently, the responsivity of the device decreased with an increase in the light power density. As another vital aspect for practical applications, the photodiode stability was examined as a function of the operating time. As can be observed from Fig. 6f, the photoresponse of the device decreased slightly by 5.5% after 10 weeks of storage in a closed container. Furthermore, the photodiode showed outstanding reproducibility with 5.7% current degeneration upon exposure to continuous turning on–off for 100 cycles. Compared to the ZnO-based UV photodetectors reported previously, the ZnO:GQD photodiode displayed much faster response along with superior or comparable level of both responsivity and specific detectivity for UV detection (Table 1 ).

A low-cost ZnO:GQD thin film was fabricated via a solution-processed method for application in photodiode and photoconductor UV detectors. It was found that the incorporation of appropriate amount of GQDs into ZnO was beneficial in improving the carrier transportation, which was proved by the (i) increased carrier lifetime, (ii) decreased charge transfer resistance, (iii) decreased response time, and (iv) increased photocurrent. Furthermore, it was found that the device architecture played a pivotal role in the photodetector performance. The device with the ZnO:GQD/Poly-TPD heterojunction showed excellent response speed (rise/decay time of 0.37/0.78 s) and a specific detectivity of 2.1 × 1011 Jones. Furthermore, the optimized device (in terms of the device structure and GQD concentration in ZnO:GQD) showed a stable photoresponse during 100 cycles after storing for 10 weeks. Hence, this study gives a new insight regarding how GQD sensitization in ZnO can efficiently enhance the performance of organic–inorganic p-n heterojunction UV detecting applications.

Manh Hoang Tran: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Taehyun Park: Data curation. Jaehyun Hur: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20194030202290). This research was supported by the Korea Electric Power Corporation (Grant number: R18XA02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.148222.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis on different detection mechanisms involved in ZnO-based photodetector and photodiodes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phtoluminescence nd photoconductivity of ZnS-coated ZnO nanowires

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ashok Bera, Durga Basak, Phtoluminescence nd photoconductivity of ZnS-coated ZnO nanowires, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 2010, 2, 2, 408-12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced UV detection by transparent graphene oxide/ZnO composite thin film

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Paul, R. N. Gayen, S. Biswas, S. Venkataprasad Bhat, R. Bhunia, Enhanced UV detection by transparent graphene oxide/ZnO composite thin film, RSC Adv., 2016, 6, 61661-72.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High photo-responsivity ZnO UV detectors fabricated by RF reactive sputtering

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. L. Zhang, K. S. Hui, K. N. Hui, High photo-responsivity ZnO UV detectors fabricated by RF reactive sputtering, Mater. Res. Bull., 2013, 48, 305-09.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optoelectronic properties of solution grown ZnO n-p or p-n core-shell nanowire arrays

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ken C. Pradel, Zhong Lin Wang, et. al., Optoelectronic properties of solution grown ZnO n-p or p-n core-shell nanowire arrays, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 2016, 8, 7, 4287-91.
